_patch_meta.session_ref: M09 Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 109 verses across 17 terms

set.analysis_note: The peoples' obedience is a willing, inner yielding of allegiance directed toward Judah's coming ruler, framed as a future gathering of nations under his sovereign authority.
The willing inner disposition originates in the heart; a generous, freely-moved heart is the inner condition that produces the outward act of bringing an offering.
A willing heart is explicitly named as the inner source driving both men and women to bring gold offerings; the action flows from an uncoerced, inwardly-motivated readiness.
Humbling is located in the heart, described here as 'uncircumcised' until broken; it is the inner turning away from hardness that must precede making amends and receiving restoration.
Ahab's humbling is a visible, relational act directed toward God; God perceives it as genuine enough to produce inner contrition, and it directly affects divine response—delaying judgment.

Humbling is paired with a penitent heart, located in the inner person; it is expressed through weeping and tearing clothes, and God confirms he heard it, showing it reached the divine relational register.
Willingness here is an inner readiness coupled with skill; it describes the person's disposition toward service rather than compulsion, enabling wholehearted participation in the temple work.

Humbling oneself is the first in a series of inner and relational acts—praying, seeking God's face, turning from wickedness—that together constitute the inner reorientation God requires before he forgives.
The princes and king humble themselves and confess God's righteousness; the humbling is a corporate inner acknowledgment of guilt that averts further divine judgment.

God observes the inner act of humbling and responds by withdrawing destruction; the verse shows humbling as perceived by God in the inner person and directly effective in altering his judicial action.
Individual humbling turns away God's wrath from complete destruction; the inner act functions as the decisive factor that limits divine judgment and restores relative well-being.
Here God is the subject humbling Judah as a nation—imposed lowering as consequence of Ahaz's unfaithfulness; it is an external divine act rather than an inward voluntary posture.
A willing heart is the inner condition distinguishing those who bring burnt offerings beyond the required sacrifices; it marks spontaneous, internally-motivated generosity in worship.
Some men from northern tribes humbled themselves and traveled to Jerusalem; the inner yielding overcame tribal or political resistance, enabling obedient response to Hezekiah's invitation.
Hezekiah's humbling is explicitly a response to the pride of his heart; the inner act of self-lowering directly addresses an identified inner fault, and it averts divine wrath.
In extreme distress Manasseh humbles himself greatly before God; the inner act is born of crisis and involves entreating divine favor, showing humbling as the heart's turn toward God under pressure.

Manasseh's humbling is noted as a turning point in his biography, contrasted with his earlier sin and faithlessness; the verse marks it as a datable inner change recorded for its spiritual significance.
Amon's failure to humble himself is the inner opposite of his father Manasseh's repentance; the absence of humbling is directly linked to increasing guilt, showing it as a necessary inner condition.
Josiah's humbling is paired with a tender heart; together they describe an inner responsiveness to God's word that expresses itself in weeping, and God explicitly acknowledges hearing the inner cry.
Zedekiah's refusal to humble himself before the prophet is framed as the inner root of his evil; it shows that humbling before God's word is the expected inner posture, and its absence condemns.
The verse calls for the proud to be brought low and the wicked trodden down; humbling here is not voluntary inner submission but forcible divine lowering of the proud as a judicial act.

The contrite spirit is crushed inwardly and broken in heart; this verse locates the characteristic in the spirit and heart, and names God's nearness and saving action as the direct response to that inner state.

The psalmist asks God to uphold him with a willing spirit; willingness is located in the spirit and is presented as a divine gift needed to sustain joyful, freely-offered devotion after moral failure.
Israel was brought into subjection under enemies' power; the humbling is external and coercive—a consequence of unfaithfulness—rather than a voluntary inner posture.

God bows hearts down through hard labor so people fall with no helper; the humbling acts directly on the inner faculty of the heart, using external affliction to produce inner collapse and dependence.

The humble person possesses wisdom as an inner quality; humility is placed in structural contrast with pride that brings disgrace, locating it as the inner disposition that opens a person to true understanding.
Obedience to a mother is the expected inner response that the mocking eye scorns; its absence in one who despises parental authority is marked as a fatal moral failure deserving severe consequence.

Contrition is paired with lowliness and located in the spirit; God dwells with the contrite and lowly spirit and revives both spirit and heart, showing contrition as the inner condition that draws God's reviving presence.
Walking humbly with God is named as a comprehensive inner orientation of the whole life before God, set alongside doing justice and loving kindness as the three things God requires of humans.

The verse describes Jesus directing his twelve disciples through instruction; it does not evidence inner-being content for this term—it is a narrative transition note about completed authoritative teaching.
Jesus locates lowliness explicitly in the heart as a self-description; it is paired with gentleness and presented as the inner quality that makes his yoke restful for souls, inviting imitation.

Humbling oneself like a child is presented as the inner disposition that defines greatness in God's kingdom; it contrasts implicitly with self-exaltation and involves a willed, downward self-assessment.
Self-humbling is the inner act that leads to divine exaltation, set in direct antithesis to self-exaltation; the verse shows the inner posture as determinative of one's ultimate standing before God.

Mary's lowly estate is the outward condition of her servant status that God looked upon; the verse presents lowliness as the social-spiritual condition from which God lifts her, making it the context for divine favor.

God exalts those of humble estate by bringing down the mighty; lowliness here is a social and spiritual condition that stands against worldly power, and God's reversal validates it as the inner-relational posture he honors.

Mountains and hills being made low is physical-prophetic imagery from Isaiah; this verse does not evidence inner-being content for the term—it is a landscape metaphor for preparing the way of the Lord.
John directs tax collectors to collect only what is authorized; this verse does not evidence inner-being content for the term—it is a practical command about conduct rather than an inner disposition.
Self-humbling is contrasted with self-exaltation as the inner choice that determines divine outcome; the one who inwardly takes the lower place will be lifted up by God.
The servant does what is commanded without expectation of thanks; this verse does not directly evidence inner-being content for the term—it is a rhetorical question about the master-servant dynamic.

Having done all that was directed, servants are to regard themselves as unworthy and merely dutiful; the verse ties obedience to commanded duties with an inner posture of self-assessment that rejects merit.

The tax collector who humbled himself before God goes home justified; humbling is the inner posture of self-lowering before God that enables justification, contrasted with the Pharisee's self-exaltation.
Many priests became obedient to the faith; obedience here is a volitional inner act of submission to the gospel message, representing a decisive reorientation of will and allegiance.

God directed Moses in how to make the tabernacle according to a heavenly pattern; the verse does not evidence inner-being content for the term—it describes authoritative divine instruction about a physical structure.

Christ's humiliation is a condition of being denied justice and having life taken away; the verse presents lowliness as an imposed, suffering state experienced in the deepest relational and existential dimension.
The disciples urge Peter to come without delay; the term names an absence of hesitation or reluctance, pointing to immediate, unimpeded responsiveness as the inner-volitional quality expected of him.
Claudius commanded all Jews to leave Rome; this verse does not evidence inner-being content for the term—it records an external imperial edict with no inner-disposition dimension.
Paul served the Lord with all humility amid tears and trials; humility is an inner quality pervading his whole manner of ministry, sustained through suffering rather than deflated by it.

Obedience of faith is the inner-relational response to the gospel that Paul's apostleship aims to produce among the nations; it is directed toward God's name and involves volitional submission to the proclaimed word.

Christ's obedience is set against Adam's disobedience as the act that makes many righteous; the inner dimension is the willing compliance of the will that has cosmic, redemptive consequence for all who are in him.
Sin's reign produces obedience to bodily passions; the verse locates this obedience in the mortal body's desires and frames it as an inner yielding of the will that believers are commanded to resist.

Obedience here names a state of inner allegiance: whoever one presents oneself to as a slave defines whose authority rules the will, either sin's (producing death) or a righteousness-oriented obedience that reshapes the person's direction.

The act of obeying discloses an inner allegiance: whoever the person obeys becomes their master, so the will's habitual yielding to sin or to righteousness determines the person's enslaved condition and ultimate end.

Obedience here is explicitly located in the heart—it is not merely outward compliance but a willing inner conformity to a pattern of teaching, marking a genuine transformation of the person's core disposition from sin's slavery.

Obeying the gospel is equated with believing it; the verse exposes that many heard but did not inwardly yield, revealing that the inner act of faith and the act of obedience are inseparable responses of the will.

The lowly here are those of humble station or spirit; associating with them is contrasted with haughtiness, so the verse frames lowliness as the contour against which proud self-regard is defined, calling for relational solidarity rather than social hierarchy.

Obedience is the goal Christ accomplishes through Paul among the Gentiles—a transformed response of the whole person (word and deed) that orients life toward God, describing an inner reorientation rather than mere external conformity.

The Romans' obedience is publicly known and a cause for rejoicing, showing it is a characteristic disposition of their community's will; Paul then redirects that yielding disposition toward discernment of good and innocence from evil.

Obedience of faith is the inner-being goal of the gospel's disclosure to all nations—faith itself issues in a yielding of the whole person to God, so obedience names the will's trustful submission as the culminating response to revealed mystery.

The directing here is Paul's apostolic rule shaping how believers order their lives; the verse does not primarily address inner humility or submission but rather the authority by which communal conduct is regulated across all churches.

The Lord's directing here is a command governing material provision for gospel proclaimers; the verse focuses on divine authority structuring community practice rather than on any inner disposition of humility or submission.

Paul's directing covers practical church order matters deferred to a personal visit; the verse concerns apostolic authority organizing communal conduct rather than evidencing an inner characteristic of meekness or submission.

Paul directed the Galatian churches and now applies the same instruction to Corinth; the verse is about apostolic ordering of a collection rather than an inner disposition of humility, though it implies that recipients are expected to follow direction.

The downcast (tapeinos) here are those pressed down by outward affliction; God's comfort answers that lowly, dejected condition of spirit, locating the characteristic in a state of inner distress that God actively meets and lifts.

Titus's deepened affection is stirred by recalling the Corinthians' obedience—their receptive, reverent welcome of him with fear and trembling—showing obedience as an inner disposition expressed through posture and attitude, not merely compliance.

Submission here flows directly from confession of the gospel, making it a faith-rooted inner yielding to Christ's lordship that then expresses itself outwardly in generous giving and glorifying God through communal approval.

Paul describes himself as lowly in personal presence but bold at a distance; the verse contrasts a perceived inner meekness or self-effacement face-to-face against his confident authority in writing, framing humility as a relational posture others misjudge.

Obedience to Christ is the outcome of taking every thought captive, locating it in the mind and will: the inner battle against proud reasoning is won when all cognitive allegiance is surrendered to Christ's authority.

Obedience here is presented as a corporate inner state that must be complete before discipline is applied; its completeness implies that the community's willing submission must be fully realized, contrasting obedience against disobedience as rival inner dispositions.

Paul voluntarily lowered himself—taking a menial, unpaid role—so that the Corinthians could be elevated; the self-humbling is a willed, deliberate inner act of self-diminishment in service of another's benefit.

God humbling Paul before the congregation would produce grief and mourning within him over unrepentant sin; the humbling is an inwardly painful experience of being brought low by the moral failure of those he loves.

Paul refuses to yield in submission even briefly to false teachers, showing submission as a decisive act of the will that can be withheld; here non-submission protects gospel truth, making the act of yielding or not yielding a matter of inner conscience and conviction.
The law was directed or ordained through angels by a mediator; the verse concerns the law's mediated, subordinate status rather than any inner disposition of humility or submission in persons.

Humility is listed alongside gentleness, patience, and love-bearing as a foundational inner disposition enabling corporate harmony; it is presented as a quality of character that must characterize every relational interaction in the body.

Children's obedience to parents is grounded in the Lord—making it a theologically motivated act of the will, not mere social compliance; the phrase 'in the Lord' frames it as an inner orientation of conscience toward divine right.

Obedience to earthly masters is to be rendered with fear, trembling, and a sincere heart as to Christ, locating the act in the inner person: genuine, undivided intention of heart rather than outward performance or people-pleasing.

Humility is defined as counting others more significant than oneself—a willful inner reordering of self-estimation that directly opposes conceit and selfish ambition, making it a cognitive and affective act of the will.

Christ's self-humbling was a willed, downward movement of the self—taking on human form and accepting death—showing humility as a volitional, self-emptying inner act that drives outward obedience to the Father even to the extreme of the cross.

Their obedience is a sustained inner disposition ('always obeyed') that must persist even more rigorously in Paul's absence, when no external accountability supports it; this reveals obedience as rooted in conscience and fear of God rather than social pressure.

The 'lowly body' names the present degraded, mortal physical condition of believers; the term points to an objective creaturely lowliness that will be transformed by Christ's power into conformity with his glorious body.

Being brought low (tapeinoō) is a circumstantial, experiential state Paul has passed through and learned to navigate; here the inner quality is a cultivated contentment enabling him to endure lowness without loss of peace or stability.

The asceticism being condemned is labeled a false humility; the verse exposes that outward self-denial can mask inner pride ('puffed up'), so genuine humility is implicitly distinguished from a performance that inflates the sensuous mind.

Self-imposed asceticism has an appearance of wisdom and humility but cannot restrain the flesh; the verse distinguishes performed self-lowering from any genuine inner transformation, showing that outward severity does not touch the inner disposition.

Humility is a garment to be deliberately 'put on' by God's chosen, placing it in the will as a chosen disposition alongside compassion, kindness, and meekness—together forming the inner character-wardrobe of the renewed person.

Children's obedience to parents in everything is grounded in pleasing the Lord, making the inner motive theological; the act of obedience is shaped by the child's awareness of God's pleasure as the deepest reason for compliance.

Obedience must flow from sincerity of heart and fear of the Lord rather than eye-service or people-pleasing, grounding the act firmly in the inner person: genuine motive, undivided conscience, and God-ward reverence replace performative outward compliance.

Failing to obey the gospel is paired with not knowing God and brings divine judgment; the verse frames disobedience as a fundamental inner rejection of God's revealed authority, not merely ignorance, with catastrophic relational and eschatological consequences.

Refusing to obey Paul's letter warrants communal shaming; the verse treats obedience to apostolic instruction as an expected disposition of the will, and its absence reveals an inner refusal that the community's response is meant to awaken to repentance.

Dignity (semnotēs) is paired with godliness as the inner quality characterizing a peaceful, quiet life under good governance; it names a sober, weighty seriousness of character that shapes how believers carry themselves in public life.

A woman's submissiveness in learning is to be complete and accompanied by quietness; the verse locates submission as the inner posture of the learner—a willing, whole-hearted receptivity rather than resistance or self-assertion.

The overseer's dignity characterizes how he maintains order in his household; it is an inner gravity or seriousness that produces orderly, respectful compliance in his children, functioning as the character quality from which household governance flows.

Children's submissiveness in the overseer's household reflects the inner disposition of yielding to parental authority; it is evidence of the father's character and household order, showing submission as a shaped, household-cultivated inner quality.

Paul's directing Titus concerns apostolic instruction shaping church order; the verse focuses on legitimate authority and obedience to it rather than evidencing an inner disposition of humility or meekness in the one being directed.

Dignity is listed alongside integrity as a quality of Titus's teaching character; it names a gravitas and moral seriousness that must be visible in how he presents himself, serving as an inner quality that authenticates his instruction.

Paul's confidence in Philemon's obedience anticipates a willing, generous response that exceeds what is merely required; obedience here is presented as a reliable inner disposition of the will oriented toward doing good beyond the minimum.

The high priest can deal gently with the erring because he himself shares human weakness; the gentleness is an inner capacity for measured, compassionate restraint grounded in shared creaturely vulnerability rather than detachment or severity.

Christ learned obedience through suffering, revealing it as a quality acquired through the will's costly engagement with hardship—not a natural given but a trained disposition of the inner person shaped by enduring pain in submission to the Father.

Christ becomes salvation's source only to those who obey him; obedience is the ongoing inner orientation of trust and submission that connects the believer to salvation, making it a condition of the will inseparable from receiving Christ's benefit.

Abraham obeyed the call to go out without knowing the destination; his obedience is explicitly faith-driven—a willed inner response to divine command that overrides the need for comprehension, trusting God's word more than personal knowledge.

The lowly brother is to boast in his exaltation, showing that social or material lowness does not define his true standing; the verse reframes the inner experience of lowliness by locating dignity in spiritual elevation rather than earthly condition.

The rich person is to boast in humiliation—the lowering of status as fleeting as grass—locating lowliness as a corrective inner posture that recognizes creaturely transience and deflates pride rooted in wealth or position.

The lowly person stands in contrast to the proud and receives God's grace; the inner disposition of lowliness opens the will toward God rather than self-assertion, making the person receptive to divine favor.
Humbling oneself is a volitional act directed before the Lord, involving the will actively bowing the self downward; this inner submission before God is the condition God meets with exaltation.
Obedience here is the goal-state toward which the Spirit's sanctifying work directs believers—an inner orientation of the will toward Jesus Christ, paired with the cleansing of his blood.

Obedience defines the believer's identity as 'obedient children' and shapes the will against conformity to former passions; it stands as the structural opposite of yielding to ignorance-driven desire.
Obedience to the truth is credited with purifying the soul itself—it reaches the deepest inner faculty and produces the genuine affective fruit of sincere, earnest love from a pure heart.

Sarah's obedience to Abraham is expressed through speech and inward disposition (calling him lord), and readers are called to mirror this by doing good without fear—courage and will sustaining the obedient stance.

The humble person, as the one who receives God's grace, is defined against the proud; the lowliness in view is a relational disposition—toward both fellow believers and God—positioned as the inner ground for divine favor.

Humility is here a garment worn toward one another—an active, willed disposition of mutual self-lowering within the community, motivated by God's resistance of the proud and his grace given to the humble.

Humbling oneself is a deliberate act of the will placing the self under God's mighty hand; the inner posture of self-lowering before God's sovereign power is the condition for being exalted at the proper time.
